ontology.foodon_label: mixed dish (grain-based with meat and vegetables)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for a customisable chicken grain bowl; FOODON_00002473 (mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Specific grain bowl subtype not yet represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — grain-based with poultry and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (composite dish) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for a customisable chicken grain bowl with variable toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite customisable dish type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl), comprising roughly 100–120 g cooked chicken, 120–140 g cooked mixed grains, and three vegetable or legume toppings totalling approximately 90–100 g, plus sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Grain Bowl (3 Toppings) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for 'Chicken grain bowl (3 toppings)' (cr_id F08010002) with confidence 0.95. All per-100 g values preserved from the source record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.20 g/100 g using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled chicken breast with mixed grains (USDA NDB 05064 and 20006 composite) as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain and chicken composite dish analogous estimate based on mixed grain GI (GI ~45, Atkinson et al., 2021)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a customisable chicken grain bowl. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed grains / brown rice composite (GI range 42–50). Chicken and non-starchy vegetable toppings contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 9.55 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 15.1 per 350 g serving. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 254 mg/100 g (889 mg per 350 g serving) is borderline-high; sauce selection and portion size are the primary modifiable sodium drivers.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side and choose vinaigrette or olive oil over soy-based or teriyaki sauces, as a full 350 g serving with standard sauce can deliver approximately 890 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45) from mixed grains, 2.1 g/100 g dietary fibre, and 8.69 g/100 g protein collectively moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish as part of a balanced meal plan; specifying brown rice or mixed grains over white rice, choosing low-sugar sauces, and monitoring total portion size (ideally ≤300 g) will help maintain postprandial blood glucose within target range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean grilled chicken, whole grains, and vegetable toppings provide a favourable macronutrient profile with moderate fat (4.86 g/100 g) and estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.2 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease can consume this dish regularly when sauce sodium is controlled; the combination of whole grains, lean protein, and fibre-rich vegetable toppings aligns with AHA dietary guidelines for heart health, and choosing olive oil-based dressings further improves the unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole grains, colourful vegetable toppings, and dietary fibre at 2.1 g/100 g support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis benefit from choosing anti-inflammatory toppings such as broccoli, edamame, or carrot and requesting an olive oil-based dressing to maximise monounsaturated fatty acid intake; nightshade toppings (tomato, bell pepper) should be avoided by individuals who report sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Chicken Grain Bowl (3 Toppings) is a contemporary health-casual dish widely available in Singapore's café and health-food restaurant scene. It consists of cooked chicken — typically grilled or roasted — served over a base of mixed whole grains such as brown rice, quinoa, or farro, accompanied by a customer's choice of three toppings (commonly roasted or raw vegetables, legumes such as edamame, or pickled items) and a sauce. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit, making its final nutritional profile variable depending on topping and sauce selection. Per 100 g, it delivers 117 kcal, 8.69 g protein, 4.86 g fat, 9.55 g carbohydrate, 2.1 g dietary fibre, and 254 mg sodium, as reported by the Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo database (cr_id F08010002). The mixed grain base provides a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and is classified as low-calorie. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension (with sauce moderation), and cardiovascular disease, owing to its lean protein content, whole-grain carbohydrate base, and fibre from vegetable toppings. The primary nutritional caution is sodium, which can rise substantially depending on sauce choice; soy-based and teriyaki sauces are the main sodium contributors. Known in Chinese as 鸡肉谷物碗, in Malay as Mangkuk Bijirin Ayam, in Tamil as சிக்கன் தானிய கிண்ணம், and in Hindi as चिकन अनाज बाउल.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F08010002) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed grains, which is the closest approved GI source; this introduces minor uncertainty in GI only. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and documented accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite customisable dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F08010002, and no sufficiently close composite equivalent exists in USDA FoodData Central to support reliable estimation for these fields. potassium_mg is estimated at 220 mg/100 g based on the input record health suitability narrative reference and USDA SR Legacy composite data for grilled chicken with mixed grains; this value should be confirmed against a direct HPB or USDA measurement. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 1.20 g/100 g from USDA SR Legacy composite (grilled chicken breast + mixed grains) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. vitamin_c_mg is estimated at 5 mg/100 g based on the input record health suitability narrative reference; this should be confirmed. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed grain / brown rice composite; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. This is a customisable dish; actual nutrient values will vary depending on topping and sauce selection. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 mandatory Singaporean dish naming requirements, as the dish is sold and catalogued in Singapore.
